docs.title: Civic Accountability Toolkit
docs.text: Evidence-first toolkit for organizing public facts, linking incidents to sources, and sending fact-based oversight requests.

docs.title: Start in three steps
docs.text:
1. Open How to Use.
  2. Review one incident and its linked evidence.
  3. Use a template to contact the correct official channel.

docs.title: Before you contribute
docs.text:
• Read the sourcing standard.
  • Keep findings separate from allegations.
  • Prefer official records, court orders, and document-backed reporting.

docs.title: Good contributions
docs.text:
• Adding a source note with clear factual extraction
  • Improving an incident timeline
  • Linking a person note to reliable evidence
  • Clarifying what is still unproved

docs.title: Avoid
docs.text:
• Unsupported accusations
  • Personal attacks
  • Doxxing or harassment
  • Copying entire news articles

docs.title: For ordinary users
docs.text:
1. Pick one incident from the incident index.
  2. Check linked evidence and source tier.
  3. Confirm what is established and what remains alleged.
  4. Choose the right recipient: House, Senate, DOJ Public Integrity, or Inspector General.
  5. Send a short, respectful, fact-based message.
  6. Record what you sent and any reply.

docs.title: For contributors
docs.text:
1. Add or improve one source-backed note.
  2. Link it to at least one incident and one issue.
  3. Mark missing evidence clearly.
  4. Run the sync script and validate links.

docs.title: Is this a legal filing?
docs.text: No. It is a public-record and oversight toolkit.

docs.title: Does this repo declare anyone guilty?
docs.text: No. It separates court findings, documented allegations, and open questions.

docs.title: Can I use this to contact Congress?
docs.text: Yes, but keep messages factual, respectful, and tied to sources.

docs.title: Can I open this in Obsidian?
docs.text: Yes. The repo is designed to work as a linked Markdown vault.

docs.title: Graph View Guide
docs.text:
Use Obsidian graph view to explore: - People ↔ Issues - People ↔ Incidents - People ↔ Agencies - Relationship notes as explicit graph edges

Start with the People Index and Graph Index dashboards.

docs.title: For ordinary citizens
docs.text:
1. Start in 00_HOME.md.
  2. Pick one incident.
  3. Read the linked evidence note.
  4. Use the citizen letter template.
  5. Send a respectful, fact-based oversight request.

docs.title: For contributors
docs.text:
1. Add a source note first.
  2. Create or update an incident note.
  3. Link the person, issue, and statute notes.
  4. Mark what is documented vs unresolved.

docs.title: For maintainers
docs.text:
• Keep titles short and consistent
  • Prefer one incident per note
  • Use source tiers honestly
  • Remove or quarantine weak sourcing

docs.title: Safe Civic Use
docs.text: This repo is for lawful civic participation.

docs.title: Allowed uses
docs.text:
• Public-record review
  • Fact-based constituent outreach
  • Oversight requests
  • Record-preservation requests
  • Obsidian knowledge management

docs.title: Not allowed
docs.text:
• Threats
  • Harassment
  • Doxxing
  • Fabricated claims
  • Presenting allegations as convictions
  • Spam or abusive outreach

docs.text:

docs.title: Use this order of preference
docs.text:
1. Official records and court orders
  2. Major outlet reporting with document support or named sourcing
  3. Expert letters, watchdogs, NGOs
  4. Opinion and commentary only as context

docs.title: Red lines
docs.text:
• Do not state a crime occurred unless the source supports that level of certainty.
  • Do not equate allegations with findings.
  • Do not use social media claims as primary proof.

docs.title: Every incident should answer
docs.text:
• What happened?
  • Who was involved?
  • What source supports this?
  • What remains unknown?

docs.text: Melania Knauss Trump is a Slovenian and American former model serving as the first lady of the United States since 2025, a role she previously held from 2017 to 2021 as the wife of Donald Trump, the 45th and 47th president of the United States. She is the first naturalized citizen and the first non-native English speaker to become first lady; the second foreign-born first lady, after Louisa Adams; the second Roman Catholic first lady, after Jacqueline Kennedy; and the second to hold the position nonconsecutively, after Frances Cleveland.

docs.title: Wikipedia Summary: Robert F. Kennedy Jr.
Summary
docs.text: Robert Francis Kennedy Jr., also known by his initials RFK Jr., is an American politician, environmental lawyer, author, conspiracy theorist, and anti-vaccine activist serving as the 26th United States secretary of health and human services since 2025. A member of the prominent Kennedy family, he is a son of Senator and US attorney general Robert F. Kennedy and a nephew of US president John F. Kennedy and US senator Ted Kennedy.

docs.title: Source
docs.text: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert%20F.%20Kennedy%20Jr.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Russell Thurlow Vought is an American political advisor who has served as the director of the Office of Management and Budget since 2025. Vought has additionally served as the acting director of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau since February 2025. He served as the acting administrator of the United States Agency for International Development from August to November 2025. Vought served as the Office of Management and Budget's director from 2020 to 2021 and as its deputy director, as well as its acting director, from 2018 to 2020.

docs.title: Source
docs.text: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russ%20Vought

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Scott Kenneth Homer Bessent is an American businessman and government official serving since 2025 as the 79th United States secretary of the treasury. Bessent was a partner at Soros Fund Management (SFM) and founded Key Square Group, a global macro investment firm. He is the first openly gay person to lead the U.S. Treasury Department, the first openly gay U.S. Senate-confirmed Cabinet member in a Republican administration, and the second openly gay U.S. Senate-confirmed Cabinet secretary.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Melania Knauss Trump is a Slovenian and American former model serving as the first lady of the United States since 2025, a role she previously held from 2017 to 2021 as the wife of Donald Trump, the 45th and 47th president of the United States. She is the first naturalized citizen and the first non-native English speaker to become first lady; the second foreign-born first lady, after Louisa Adams; the second Roman Catholic first lady, after Jacqueline Kennedy; and the second to hold the position nonconsecutively, after Frances Cleveland.

docs.text: Robert Francis Kennedy Jr., also known by his initials RFK Jr., is an American politician, environmental lawyer, author, conspiracy theorist, and anti-vaccine activist serving as the 26th United States secretary of health and human services since 2025. A member of the prominent Kennedy family, he is a son of Senator and US attorney general Robert F. Kennedy and a nephew of US president John F. Kennedy and US senator Ted Kennedy.

docs.text: Russell Thurlow Vought is an American political advisor who has served as the director of the Office of Management and Budget since 2025. Vought has additionally served as the acting director of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau since February 2025. He served as the acting administrator of the United States Agency for International Development from August to November 2025. Vought served as the Office of Management and Budget's director from 2020 to 2021 and as its deputy director, as well as its acting director, from 2018 to 2020.

docs.text: Scott Kenneth Homer Bessent is an American businessman and government official serving since 2025 as the 79th United States secretary of the treasury. He is the first openly gay person to lead the U.S. Treasury Department, the first openly gay U.S. Senate-confirmed Cabinet member in a Republican administration, and the second openly gay U.S. Senate-confirmed Cabinet secretary.

docs.title: Establishment Clause note
docs.text: Government actions relating to religion may raise constitutional issues if they advance, favor, or entangle government with religion in unlawful ways.

docs.title: Plain-English note
docs.text: Protects due process against federal government action.

docs.title: In this repo
docs.text: Used where federal action allegedly imposed penalties or adverse action without fair procedures.

docs.title: Plain-English note
docs.text: Protects speech, press, religion, assembly, and petition.

docs.title: In this repo
docs.text: Used for incident mapping where official actions may burden speech, press access, or retaliation concerns.

docs.title: Do not overclaim
docs.text: A policy dispute is not automatically a First Amendment violation; facts and court rulings matter.

docs.title: Plain-English note
docs.text: Includes the Citizenship Clause and equal protection / due process guarantees.

docs.title: In this repo
docs.text: Used mainly for the birthright citizenship litigation mapping.

docs.title: Immigration due process note
docs.text: Administrative and constitutional protections can apply in immigration adjudication and status determinations.

docs.title: War Crimes Act note
docs.text: This note is for very high-threshold allegations only. Public reporting alone is not enough to conclude criminal war-crimes liability.

docs.title: Plain-English note
docs.text: The Constitution and federal war-powers framework allocate responsibilities between Congress and the President concerning the use of force.

docs.title: In this repo
docs.text: Used to frame oversight questions about authorization, reporting, and legal basis.
